title: Irregular verbs
subtitle: хотеть, мочь, идти, and the verb быть

vocabulary.exampleMeaning: I have a book. (lit: at me there is a book)

hook.text: Three verbs in today's lesson appear in almost every Russian sentence — хотеть (to want), мочь (can), идти (to go). They're irregular, which means the conjugations don't fit neatly into either class you've learned. You can't derive them from rules — you have to know them. The payoff is immediate: these are the most useful verbs at A1 level.

why.text: хотеть is uniquely irregular — it uses first-conjugation endings for singular (хочу, хочешь, хочет) but second-conjugation endings for plural (хотим, хотите, хотят). This split-conjugation pattern is rare in Russian and this is the most common example. мочь has a consonant change throughout: Г→Ж. идти is completely irregular in stem.

cards.title: хотеть — to want (split conjugation)
cards.body: хотеть switches conjugation class mid-paradigm. Singular forms use first-conjugation endings. Plural forms use second-conjugation endings. The consonant also changes: Т→Ч in я, ты, он forms.

cards.title: мочь — can / to be able to
cards.body: мочь has a consonant change throughout: Г→Ж in all forms. The stress also shifts. Learn all six forms — they appear constantly in requests, offers, and expressing ability.

cards.title: идти — to go (on foot, one direction)
cards.body: идти uses a different stem (ид-) in conjugation. It means 'to be going' right now, in one specific direction, on foot. Don't use it for habitual 'going' — that's ходить (covered later).

cards.title: быть — to be; есть — there is
cards.body: быть (to be) is absent in present tense (you already know this from Unit 2). But it reappears in future tense: буду, будешь, будет, будем, будете, будут. The present-tense remnant есть means 'there is / there are' — used in У меня есть (I have, literally 'at me there is').

examples.meaning: I have a book. (at me there is a book)

questions.explanation: хотеть: я хочу. Т→Ч mutation, first conjugation ending -у.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I have a book' in Russian?
questions.explanation: У меня есть книга. = at me there is a book. This is the standard Russian construction for 'to have'.
мочь: Г→Ж mutation throughout. ты + -ешь = можешь.

cards.title: To have — У + genitive + есть
cards.body: Russian has no verb 'to have'. Instead: У [person in genitive] есть [thing in nominative]. У меня (at me) есть (there is) книга = I have a book. To negate: У меня нет книги (I don't have a book — thing goes genitive). The есть is often dropped in spoken Russian when the possession is obvious.

cards.title: идти vs ходить — direction matters
cards.body: Russian has two verbs for 'to go on foot': идти (one direction, right now) and ходить (habitual, multi-directional, or back-and-forth). You'll learn ходить in Unit 9 when verb aspects are covered. For now, use идти for 'I am going somewhere now' — you'll encounter this distinction a lot.

examples.meaning: I am going to the shop (now, one way).

examples.cyrillic: Я хожу в магазин каждый день.
examples.transliteration: Ya khozhu v magazin kazhdiy den'.
examples.meaning: I go to the shop every day (habitual).

questions.explanation: хотеть: singular uses 1st conjugation. она + Т→Ч + -ет = хочет.

questions.prompt: How do you ask 'where are you going?' (informal)?
questions.explanation: идти: ты → идёшь. куда = where to (direction).
хотеть: они uses second conjugation (-ят). они хотят.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'do you have money?' (informal)?
questions.explanation: У тебя (at you, informal) есть (there is) деньги (money)?.

questions.prompt: What is 'I am going' (on foot, right now)?

questions.explanation: куда = where to (movement toward). где = where (static location). откуда = where from.

rules: хотеть: singular uses 1st conjugation (хочу хочешь хочет), plural uses 2nd (хотим хотите хотят)
мочь: Г→Ж throughout (могу можешь может можем можете могут)
идти: я иду, ты идёшь, он идёт, мы идём, вы идёте, они идут — one-direction foot travel
To have: У + genitive + есть (У меня есть книга = I have a book)
быть is absent in present tense but used in future: буду будешь будет...
summary.nextLesson: Next: verb aspects — the perfective/imperfective distinction you already know conceptually, applied to the verbs you've learned.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I have a book'?
